description: A city that somehow does everything brilliantly — Gaudí architecture, world-class beaches, Europe's best nightlife, incredible food markets, and a creative energy that keeps you up until dawn.

neighborhoodRecs: El Born / Sant Pere — Trendy, walkable, best tapas bars and wine spots, close to Picasso Museum and Barceloneta beach.
Gràcia — Bohemian, local neighbourhood with Parisian square vibes, fewer tourists, great independent restaurants.
Eixample — Gaudí central (Sagrada Família, Casa Batlló), wide boulevards, LGBTQ+-friendly, best mid-range hotels.
Barceloneta — Beach neighbourhood, great for beach access and seafood, touristy but fun in summer.
areasToAvoid: Las Ramblas at night — pickpocket teams are professional and relentless, especially around the middle section.
El Raval south of Carrer de l'Hospital after midnight — still gentrifying, rough edges late at night.
Barceloneta beach with valuables — bag snatching from sunbathers is common, never leave anything on the sand.
scamWarnings: Shell game scams on Las Ramblas — seemingly friendly crowd watching a street game are all in on it. You will lose money.
Unlicensed taxis outside nightclubs quote fixed prices that are 5–10x the meter rate. Use the Cabify or Uber app instead.
The 'friendship bracelet' hustle — someone ties a bracelet on your wrist without asking then demands payment. Walk away firmly.
Fake police officers asking to check your wallet for counterfeit bills — real police never do this. Walk away and call 112.

airportTransfer.cheapest: Aerobus from Terminal 1 or T2 to Plaça Catalunya — €6.75 single, runs every 5 min, takes 35 min. Buy online to save queuing.
airportTransfer.fastest: Taxi from airport — €35–45 fixed rate to city centre, takes 25–35 min depending on traffic.
airportTransfer.recommended: Aerobus for solo travellers or couples — fast, cheap, drops at Plaça Catalunya which is central to everything. Taxi for families or late-night arrivals.
airportTransfer.taxiEstimate: Official taxi €35–45 fixed rate to Barcelona centre. All taxis from the airport are metered or use the fixed zone tariff.

tippingEtiquette: Tipping is not obligatory but appreciated. Round up the bill or leave €1–2 at restaurants — 10% for excellent service.
Bars and cafes: no tipping expected for quick drinks, small coins welcome if you're a regular.
Catalans speak Catalan first — greet with 'Bon dia' (morning) or 'Bona tarda' (afternoon) to earn instant goodwill.
Spanish dinner time is 9–10pm. Arriving before 8:30pm marks you as a tourist; restaurants may not even be open.
Avoid photographing people in markets (Boqueria) without asking — many vendors find it disrespectful.

dailyCostBreakdown.accommodation: Hostel dorm €22–35 | Mid-range hotel €90–160 | Boutique hotel €180–350
dailyCostBreakdown.meals: Menú del día (3-course lunch) €11–16 | Tapas dinner €20–35 | Fine dining €70+
dailyCostBreakdown.transport: Metro single €2.55 | T-Casual 10-trip card €11.35 | Taxi across city €8–14
dailyCostBreakdown.activities: Sagrada Família €26–32 | Casa Batlló €35 | Picasso Museum €12 | Camp Nou tour €26

packingTips: Anti-theft crossbody bag — pickpockets target tourists on Las Ramblas and the Metro.
Light layers for evenings — even in summer, a light jacket for after-midnight terrace bars.
Comfortable walking shoes — Barcelona's grid is very walkable but the Gothic Quarter cobblestones are tough on feet.
Swimwear and SPF 50 — Barceloneta beach is steps from the centre.
premium.bookingStrategy: Book Sagrada Família and Casa Batlló tickets 2–3 months in advance for July/August — they sell out completely. Best time to visit is May or October for weather and manageable crowds. Book accommodation in El Born or Eixample for best access.
localTips: La Boqueria is overhyped and overpriced — walk 5 minutes to Mercat de Sant Antoni or Mercat de l'Abaceria for an authentic market experience.
Vermouth (vermut) culture: Sunday vermouth at noon in El Born or Poble Sec is a Barcelona ritual — Bar Calders in Poble Sec is a local favourite.
Barceloneta beach gets impossibly crowded in July/August. Head to Mar Bella or Nova Icaria beach instead — less touristy, same sand.
Nightlife doesn't start until midnight. Clubs peak at 2–4am. Pre-drink at a bar, arrive at clubs at 1:30am minimum.
The free Bus Turístic viewpoints at Tibidabo and Park Güell are spectacular but skip the official Park Güell at peak hours — book the ticketed section online.
premium.bestTimeToVisit: May or October: 19–23°C, beaches open, events on, hotels 30% cheaper than summer peak, manageable crowds.
dayTrips: Montserrat — Dramatic mountain monastery an hour by train, extraordinary rock formations, hiking trails above the clouds.
Sitges — Gay-friendly beach town 35 min by train, beautiful old town, excellent seafood, perfect day escape from the city.
Penedès wine region — 45 min by train to Vilafranca, Catalan wine country, cava tastings, vineyard tours.
Tarragona — Roman amphitheatre and old city walls on the Costa Daurada, 1 hour by train, dramatically undervisited.
Girona — medieval Jewish quarter, colourful houses over the Onyar river, Game of Thrones filming location, 40 minutes by high-speed train.
visaInfo: EU/EEA citizens: no visa required — Spain is a full Schengen member with free movement for EU passport holders
UK citizens (post-Brexit): visa-free for up to 90 days in any 180-day Schengen period; passport must be valid for the duration of stay
US, Canadian, and Australian citizens: visa-free for up to 90 days in any 180-day Schengen period; no tourist visa required
Most other nationalities: consult the Spanish Ministry of Foreign Affairs; many nationalities require a Schengen visa obtained in advance from a Spanish consulate
The 90-day Schengen allowance is shared across all 27 Schengen member states — time in any other Schengen country counts against the same quota
currencyTips: Currency: Euro (€) — Spain is a eurozone member; no currency exchange needed if arriving from another eurozone country
Cards are widely accepted in Barcelona restaurants, hotels, and shops; contactless payment is universal at most venues
Best ATMs: use bank-branded ATMs from CaixaBank, BBVA, or Sabadell — avoid standalone tourist ATMs near Las Ramblas which charge high surcharges
Avoid: dynamic currency conversion (DCC) at card terminals — always choose to pay in euros
Cash is useful for smaller tapas bars, market stalls, and the Boqueria; keep €5–€20 notes handy
premium.simCard: Movistar, Vodafone Spain, Orange, and MásMóvil are the main Spanish carriers. Tourist SIMs with 15–20 GB data cost €10–20 and include EU roaming — buy at Barcelona El Prat Airport (BCN) Arrivals or at carrier stores on Las Ramblas and Passeig de Gràcia. For instant activation before arrival, Airalo offers Spain eSIMs.
